provenance.source_corpus: Spontaneous revegetation of a peatland in Manitoba after peat extraction: diversity of plant assemblages and restoration perspectives

source_spans.text: Spontaneous revegetation is the process by which a disturbed ecosystem that has lost its vegetation cover re-establishes a new plant cover, returning to either its initial state or another state, without human intervention.
Moss Spur has densely revegetated (163 plant species, vegetation cover of 94%) with minimal human assistance.

Water pH formed the strongest gradient among those measured and allowed a particularly good distinction between G1-Scirpus and G2-Trichophorum quadrats from those of G3-Eriophorum, which had more acidic water than the two other groups.

The hypothesis that lower pH and electrical conductivity locally favour the presence of Sphagnum-dominated peatland species, while a high pH and electrical conductivity favour fen species, is thus verified.

The hypothesis that lower pH and electrical conductivity locally favour the presence of Sphagnum-dominated peatland species, while a high pH and electrical conductivity favour fen species, is thus verified.
Many beavers have taken up residence on site and erected dams across some drainage canals, which probably reduces the effectiveness of the ditches.

The most plausible explanation for the area to be prone to flooding is that the relatively flat topography slows the peatland drainage, compounded by many drainage canals which no longer appear functional, and the drainage obstruction caused by the road and beaver dams.
